Tafseer:
The meaning of 'Nazi'at':
Although several opinions are narrated regarding 'Nazi'at' (those who extract) and 'Nashitat' (those who draw out), the dominant view is that they refer to the angels who extract the souls of the disbelievers with severity, and the souls of the believers with gentleness. Although this view is widely known, it has no solid foundation. The reports on this are purely of an explanatory nature, which do not carry the authority to be cited with confidence. There is nothing in the Qur'an to support this view either. Extracting the souls of the disbelievers and the believers is entirely a spiritual and internal matter; it is not a matter of common observation that can be presented as proof for a claim, whereas the oaths here are sworn as testimonies. Since commentators believe that an oath must be by a sacred and blessed entity, they had to interpret these words as angels. However, we repeatedly clarify that oaths in the Qur'an generally serve as testimonies for a claim, and the prominent aspect within them is serving as proof for the claim, regardless of whether the entity sworn by is sacred or not.
In our view, 'Nazi'at' refers to the violent winds that uproot trees, buildings, and fixed structures with their force. Words like 'Dhariyat', 'Mursalat', and 'Asifat' are also used in the Qur'an as attributes for these violent winds, accompanied by emphatic words like 'Dharwa', 'Urfa', and 'Asfa'. Similarly, 'Nazi'at' is used here for the violent winds that uproot trees and buildings. The word 'gharqan' (vehemently) is added to express the intensity of the action.
The depiction of the violent wind Allah Almighty unleashed as a punishment upon Aad is drawn in Surah Al-Qamar as:
(Indeed, We sent upon them a screaming wind on a day of continuous misfortune, extricating [tanzi'u] people as if they were uprooted trunks of palm trees.) (Al-Qamar, 54:19-20).
Here, the verb 'tanzi'u' (extricates/uproots) is used, from which 'Nazi'at' is used as an attribute in this Surah.

The meaning of 'Nashitat':
'Nashitat' is derived from the root 'nasht', which means performing a task with gentleness, and also releasing a knot of a rope or letting a beast free to graze. Here, the context indicates that this refers to the gentle and soft winds, just as: (And those [ships] which glide with ease) (Adh-Dhariyat, 51:3) appeared in Surah Adh-Dhariyat.
Note that although the outward nature of violent and gentle winds is different, the wonders of His power are manifest in both. In Surah Adh-Dhariyat, the context is different; therefore, the gentle wind is mentioned as an introduction to rain. Here, it is mentioned independently; therefore, it can be a source of mercy or punishment. Its being a source of mercy is clear—as the gentle movement of the wind is the source of life and comfort—but its being a mercy or punishment is entirely in Allah's authority. He can turn even the gentle wind into a punishment whenever He wills. Indeed, the subsequent account of Moses and Pharaoh confirms this: Allah saved Moses and the Children of Israel through the violent eastern wind, and made the stillness of that very wind the source of Pharaoh and his armies' destruction.

'Sabihat' is derived from 'sabh', which means swimming. Context indicates that here it is used as an attribute of the clouds. First, the relationship between the winds and clouds is interconnected; second, a clear indication is that the subsequent two attributes are connected with the conjunction 'fa', which indicates that they are attributes of 'sabihat' and are in sequential order. This rule was explained in the previous Surahs.

Reflecting on 'And those who press forward as in a race, and those who regulate the matter' (fa-s-sabiqati sabqa, fa-l-mudabbirati amra), you will see that these words express—with minor variations—what was stated in Surah Adh-Dhariyat by: (And those which glide with ease, and those who apportion the matter), and in Surah Al-Mursalat by: (And those who make a distinction, and those who deliver a reminder). We explained in those Surahs that this refers to the winds carrying the clouds, which move in accordance with Allah's command to the land He wills, and then regulate His command—serving as a mercy for some and a punishment for others, making one region wet while leaving another dry. Thus, what was stated in Surah Adh-Dhariyat as 'and those who apportion the matter' is stated here as 'and those who regulate the matter'.
Before this, 'And those who press forward as in a race' depicts the movement of the clouds in the atmosphere, as different groups of them appear to race against one another. It appears as if they are eager to execute the command of their Lord, and each desires to be the first to obey.
The subject of the oath is omitted: The subject of the oath is omitted here based on context. Various examples of this have appeared previously, such as in Surah Sad, Surah Qaf, and Surah Al-Qiyamah. Where there is no specific need, omitting it is preferred. Here, since the upheaval of the Resurrection is detailed next—which is sufficient to clarify the subject—there was no need to mention it. To unpack this omitted subject, we can assume the words from Surah Al-Mursalat: (Indeed, what you are promised is bound to occur). Thus, presenting the wonders of the violent and gentle winds and the clouds as testimony, the rebellious Quraysh are warned: do not regard the punishment you are warned of as impossible. When Allah wills to bring it, He does not need to make any special preparation. If you learn a lesson from the history of His winds and clouds, it is sufficient. There lived far more powerful nations on the earth whom God swept away like straw through His winds.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 4 and 5 was combined in the original text.]

This is not the subject of the oath, as some have assumed, but is a reminder of the Day when they will face the punishment they are warned of. Allah's messengers warned their nations of two punishments: the punishment of the Resurrection, and the punishment that befalls a nation in this world when it rejects its messenger.
Reminding of the Day of Resurrection: First, verses 6-14 portray the punishment of the Resurrection, and then verses 16-17 present the historical testimony of the punishment that befalls a nation in this world. The Resurrection is mentioned first because it is the primary, eternal, and absolute punishment of which everyone must beware. Even if the worldly punishment does not occur, there is no safety for anyone because of the Resurrection. The worldly punishment is an additional arrangement Allah is pleased to make for those who reject His messengers.
Here, a verb is omitted before 'Day'—such as: 'Remember the Day' or similar. 
'Rajifah' means the shaking or earthquake, and 'Radifah' refers to the subsequent shake following the first. The upheaval of the Resurrection, as explained elsewhere, will be completed in two blasts of the Trumpet. Here, reference is made to the effects of both blasts, showing the deniers that the Resurrection is not impossible. In just two shakes, this entire system will be devastated.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 6 and 7 was combined in the original text.]

The impact of that Day on people's hearts: While the preceding verses described the physical effects of that Day on the universe, these verses describe its effects on the souls and hearts. It is stated: on that Day, many hearts will be beating in panic, and their eyes will be humbled in fear. This describes the state of those who lived heedless of this Day, and who mocked it when warned. As for those who feared this punishment in the world without seeing it, they will be completely secure from the terror of that Day, as stated elsewhere.

In 'their eyes' (absaruha), the reference of the pronoun is 'hearts' (quloob). The heart is what represents a person's identity, and its state is reflected through every part of his body. The eye, in particular, is the mirror reflecting the most hidden states of the heart. Because of this connection, the eyes are attributed to the hearts.

Depicting the mockery of the disbelievers:
This portrays the mockery they displayed when warned of being resurrected after death.
'Hafirah' literally means footprints, but idiomatically, if you say 'so-and-so returned to his hafirah', it means he returned to his original state.
That is: when they are warned of being resurrected after death for the account, they mock and ask one another in an interrogative tone: 'O, shall we indeed be returned to our former state?'

The repetition of the interrogative in 'Even after we have become decayed bones?' (a-idha kunna 'idhaman-nakhirah) manifests their intense amazement—showing that: first, being resurrected after death is itself strange; and second, being resurrected after the bones have turned to dust is even more amazing! The meaning is: 'Who can believe such an impossible thing!'

'They say, "That, then, would be a losing return!"' (qalu tilka idhan karratun khasirah) - That is: after mocking, they adopt a serious tone and say: 'If what these preachers say is true, then this return will indeed be a source of ruin and loss!'
Although they said this in a mocking tone, it reflects their inner state: they were not completely secure in their denial of the Resurrection. They felt the weight of its proofs and feared the consequences of denial, but their desires did not allow them to reform their lives; hence, they tried to dismiss this danger with a joke, saying: 'The Resurrection is indeed a major danger, but we will see when it comes; why should we lose our sleep over it today!' This is the primary philosophy upon which the heedless live, whereas if there is even a minor chance of the Hereafter, wisdom requires a person to live keeping it in view.

That is: they can continue their vain talk and find as many difficulties as they want, but they should remember that when Allah wills to resurrect them, He will face no difficulty, nor will any delay occur. In a single shout, they will emerge from their graves and be present in the field of gathering.

'Sahirah' means a level, open plain. Here, it refers to the field of gathering. 'A single shout' refers to the second blast of the Trumpet, described in Surah Az-Zumar as:
(Then it will be blown again, and at once they will be standing, looking on) (Az-Zumar, 39:68).

Testimony to the punishment that befalls due to denying the Messenger:
In the preceding paragraph, the punishment of the Resurrection was portrayed. Now, reference is made to the historical testimony of the punishment that befell the deniers in this world. For this, the account of Moses and Pharaoh is selected, as it is the most famous testimony.
The question 'Has there reached you' (hal ataka) is intended to draw attention to the lessons of the account, and the singular address does not necessarily refer to the Prophet, but—as we repeatedly clarify—can be general. At the end of the account, He states:
(Indeed, in that is a lesson for whoever would fear [Allah]) (verse 26).

This refers to the event when Moses, returning from Madyan, reached the valley of Tuwa at the foot of Mount Tur. Seeing a fire, he went to obtain a brand or learn the way, and was addressed from a tree: 'O Moses, I am your Lord. I have chosen you for a great mission; so go to Pharaoh as My messenger and communicate My message.'
This account is narrated here in twelve brief verses, but it is the pinnacle of the Qur'an's eloquence that all stages—from Moses' commissioning to Pharaoh's drowning—are described, leaving out nothing necessary for the instruction of the addressees.

This is the message Moses was commanded to deliver to Pharaoh: 'Go to Pharaoh; indeed, he has transgressed.' 'Transgressed' (tagha) refers to his claim of being the supreme lord and his tyranny over the Children of Israel. Claiming supreme lordship in Allah's sovereignty is the greatest rebellion. To suppress this rebellion, Allah Almighty sent Moses with His specific proof, commanding him to invite him gently to the straight path first, and if he rejects, warn him of his end.

'And say, "Would you [care to] purify yourself?"' (fa-qul hal laka ila an tazakka) - That is: 'Tell him: "Do you have any desire to lead a pure life, so that I may show you the way?"' Note that this phrase contains both the utmost compassion of a sincere advisor and the majesty of a divine messenger. The meaning is: 'While your past conduct suggests no goodness can be expected from you, Allah is ever Generous. There is still room for you; if you desire to choose a pure life, I can show you the way.'

'And let me guide you to your Lord so you would fear [Him]?' (wa ahdiyaka ila rabbika fa-takhsha) - That is: 'Currently, the mania of your supreme lordship is driving you, because of which you are running blindly; but if you have any desire to listen, let me tell you who your Lord and the Lord of the worlds is, whom everyone must fear.'
Note that a pure life is produced by the fear of God, and the fear of God is produced by His correct gnosis. Moses pointed to this reality in these two phrases.

Since Moses spoke as the Messenger of Allah, informing Pharaoh that he possessed proof of his messengership, Pharaoh demanded: 'I know of no other lord besides myself; so if you have proof of your messengership, show it.' Upon his demand, Moses showed him the great sign. 'The great sign' refers to the staff. It is described as the great sign because his other miracles were implied within it and manifested through it.

The account of Pharaoh:
That is: even after seeing the miracle, Pharaoh did not accept Moses as the Messenger of Allah, but called him a magician and a fabricator, rejecting his message. 'And disobeyed' (wa 'asa) - meaning he refused to accept the invitation mentioned in verses 18-19.

That is: having heard Moses' words, he turned back to plot against him. The details of these plots exist in other places of the Qur'an. He wanted to defeat Moses and Aaron through the magicians, but consulting his courtiers, they advised: 'These two are expert magicians; exposing them to ordinary magicians will result in defeat and public humiliation. It is better to invite the most expert magicians from across the kingdom and challenge them in an open field.' They acted on this advice, but it resulted in complete failure.

This refers to the final plot Pharaoh adopted to stop Moses' growing influence. Surah Az-Zukhruf states that whenever a calamity befell Egypt, he would ask Moses: 'Pray to your Lord to remove this calamity; if He does, I will surely accept your terms.' But when the calamity was removed, he would break his promise. Because of this repeated conduct, Moses' influence grew even among the Copts. Terrified by this, Pharaoh gathered his people to restore his influence. His speech on that occasion is pointed to briefly here. Its details are in Surah Az-Zukhruf:
(And Pharaoh called out among his people; he said, 'O my people, does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me, and these rivers flowing beneath me? Do you not then see? Or am I [not] better than this one who is insignificant and can hardly explain himself clearly? Then why have there not been cast upon him bracelets of gold or angels come with him conjoined?' So he bluffed his people, and they obeyed him. Indeed, they were a defiantly disobedient people. So when they angered Us, We took retribution from them and drowned them all.) (Az-Zukhruf, 43:51-55).
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 23 and 24 was combined in the original text.]

'Nakal' means an exemplary punishment. That is: when he persisted in his rebelliousness even after seeing and hearing everything, Allah seized him with the punishment of the Hereafter and the world. In this world, he was drowned in the waves of the sea, and in the Hereafter, he will be cast into the Fire.

This states the purpose of narrating this account. It is stated: in this indeed is a great lesson for whoever would fear Allah. Although the wording is general, the reference is specifically to the Quraysh. The meaning is: if they possess any fear of God, they should learn a lesson from it, and wise is the one who learns a lesson from the end of others rather than waiting for the calamity to befall his own head.

Arguing from the signs of the heavens and earth:
In the subsequent seven verses, Allah Almighty has drawn attention to the signs of His power and providence spread throughout the heavens and earth, which bear witness that the Creator is of such great power that no task is difficult for Him. Since He can create the heaven, why should resurrecting people after they have decayed be difficult for Him? Similarly, the arrangement of His providence from the heaven to the earth bears witness that He will not leave people unaccountable; rather, He will gather everyone one Day to see who appreciated His blessings and who chose ungratefulness, and then recompense them according to their deeds. This answers the deniers' doubt mentioned in verses 10-12, and clarifies the purpose and wisdom of the Resurrection.
'Are you a more difficult creation or is the heaven? He constructed it' (a-antum ashaddu khalqan ami-s-sama'u banaha) - It is stated: if you are in doubt as to how you can be resurrected after your bones have turned to dust, reflect: is creating this vast heaven spread over your heads more difficult, or creating you? If the Creator is powerful over constructing this heaven, what other task is more difficult than that, such that He should be unable to perform it!

This points to some aspects of His power and wisdom in the heaven, showing those who regard being resurrected as impossible: 'What is there impossible for the One Whose power you witness at all times?'
'Samk' means ceiling. It is stated: He built the heaven, raised its ceiling, and proportioned it so perfectly that no one can point to any minor defect in its corners.

'And He darkened its night and extracted its bright day' (wa aghdhasha laylaha wa akhraja duhaha) - That is: initially there was only darkness; it was, as indicated in Surah Fussilat, in the form of smoke. But then Allah Almighty darkened its night and extracted its bright day, subjecting both to a specified system. The meaning is: the God Who can darken this world, can He not recreate it after it has decayed? Is the first task more difficult or the second?

After the signs of the heaven, attention is drawn to the earth and its signs, aiming—as noted—to highlight His power and providence: 'The God Who spread the earth for you, extracted its water and pasture, and fixed mountains in it to keep it stable, can He find it difficult to recreate you?' Since He did all of this without facing any difficulty, why should recreating you be difficult for Him!
A grammatical question and its answer: The words 'And the earth after that He spread' (wa-l-arda ba'da dhalika dahaha) raise a question: was the earth created after the heaven? This question arises because in Surah Fussilat, verse 11, after describing the creation of the earth and its signs, He states: (Then He directed Himself to the heaven while it was smoke), which suggests the heaven was created after the earth. We answered this under Fussilat; refer to it for details.
Briefly, remember that the Qur'an has described the construction of the heavens and earth as a building—where the heaven is the ceiling and the earth is the floor. When planning a building, both are planned together, but during construction, work may proceed on the floor at one stage and on the ceiling at another. Thus, 'after that He spread' can refer to a specific phase of construction when the earth was spread after the heaven was established. The construction of this vast universe is beyond the comprehension of our limited intellect; its details can be encompassed only by the comprehensive knowledge of Allah.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30, 31, 32, and 33 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30, 31, 32, and 33 was combined in the original text.]
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 30, 31, 32, and 33 was combined in the original text.]

'Al-Tammat-ul-Kubra' means the great calamity or upheaval, referring to the Resurrection. Following this, the response to the conditional clause is omitted based on context. Examples of this have appeared previously. The meaning is: when that great calamity occurs, this heaven and earth will be completely devastated.

This 'Day' is the adverb of time for the omitted response—showing that on that Day, man will remember what he strove for. Today, in his heedlessness, he has no concern for what he is doing, and mocks when warned; but on that Day, he will realize what earnings he has brought.

Hell is ready, only the veil needs to be lifted: Today, they regard the Hereafter and Hell as impossible, but on that Day, Hell will be made visible to those who are to face it. 'Made visible' means it is completely ready; only the veil needs to be lifted. As soon as it is lifted, it will be before those who refused to believe in it without seeing it.
'For him who sees' (liman yara) - those whose deeds have made them deserving of it will see it. As for the righteous servants who feared it in the unseen, Allah will keep them far from it, and they will not even hear its sound.

It is stated: whoever transgressed in the world and preferred the life of this world over the Hereafter, his place of return will be Hell, from which he will never emerge. Pharaoh's transgression was mentioned in verse 17.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 37, 38, and 39 was combined in the original text.]
In 'Indeed, Hell will be the refuge' (fa-inna-l-jaheema hiya-l-ma'wa), there is an element of restriction—showing that for them, there will be only Hell, and no other path will open.

In contrast, those who feared presenting themselves before their Lord and prevented their soul from following its desires, their place of return will be Paradise, of which they will never be deprived.
'Who feared the position of his Lord' (man khafa maqama rabbihi) refers to fearing the presentation before Him, described in Surah Al-Mutaffifeen as:
(Do they not think that they will be resurrected, for a tremendous Day—the Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds?) (Al-Mutaffifeen, 83:4-6).
This very fear is what prevents the soul from following its desires; without this fear, nothing can stop it.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 40 and 41 was combined in the original text.]

Comfort for the Prophet:
This and the subsequent final verses of the Surah comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): pay no attention to those who ask you about the time of the Resurrection to mock you. Your duty is only to warn people of it so that those who wish to take heed may do so. As for those who want to see it to believe, or demand to know its exact time, satisfying them is not your responsibility. They have made this demand merely as an excuse for denial.
'Ayyana mursaha' - they ask about the time of its arrival. 'Ayyana' is used for questioning about the future; therefore, its translation is 'when', not 'where'.
'Mursa' means anchoring. This word contains a form of mockery: 'We have been hearing this warning for so long, but it has not arrived; when will its ship anchor at our shore! our eyes have grown tired waiting!'

It is stated: what concern do you have with its mention? Its ultimate knowledge belongs only to your Lord. You did not come to inform them of the exact day of its arrival. Since its knowledge is with your Lord alone, why should you pry into it! Leave it to Allah, Who alone possesses its true knowledge.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 42, 43, and 44 was combined in the original text.]
Those who want to see it to believe are not your responsibility. Leave them to their state. Your warning will benefit only those who believe in it in the unseen and fear it.

That is, if they are not shown its arrival or its date is not specified, let them not assume it is so distant that they need not worry. On the Day they see it, they will feel as if they had lived in this world only for an afternoon or its morning.
